subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given: dydx=x+y-2x-y

Now, finding the intersection point of x+y-2=0 and x-y=0.

⇒x+x-2=0, x=y

⇒x=1, y=1

⇒x,y≡1,1

Let, x=X+1, y=Y+1

⇒dYdX=X+YX-Y

Putting, Y=tX

⇒dYdX=t+XdtdX

⇒1+t1-t=t+XdtdX

⇒1+t-t+t21-t=XdtdX

⇒1XdX=1-t1+t2dt

⇒∫1XdX=∫1-t1+t2dt

⇒logX=tan-1t-12log1+t2+C

⇒logx-1=tan-1y-1x-1-12log1+y-1x-12+C

It is given that the solution curve passes through 2,1.

⇒log2-1=tan-11-12-1-12log1+1-12-12+C

⇒0=tan-10-12log1+C

⇒C=0

⇒α=1, β=2

⇒5β+α=11.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& y^2 d x+\left(x-\frac{1}{y}\right) d y=0 \\
& y^2 d x=\left(\frac{1}{y}-x\right) d y \\
& \Rightarrow y^2 \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{1}{y}-x \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{y^2}=\frac{1}{y^3} \\
& \text { I.F. }=e^{\int \frac{1}{y^2} d y}=e^{\frac{-1}{y}}
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Solution is
$x e^{\frac{-1}{y}}=\int e^{-\frac{1}{y}} \times \frac{1}{y^3} d y+C$
Let $\frac{-1}{y}=t$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{1}{y^2} d y=d t \\
& \Rightarrow x e^{-\frac{1}{y}}=-\int e^t t d t+C \\
& \Rightarrow x e^{-\frac{1}{y}}=-e^t(t-1)+C \\
& \Rightarrow x e^{-\frac{1}{y}}=-e^{\frac{-1}{y}}\left(\frac{-1}{y}-1\right)+C
\end{aligned}$
$x(1)=1$
$\Rightarrow e^{-1}=-e^{-1}(-2)+C$
$\Rightarrow C=-e^{-1}$
$\Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{y}+1-e^{-1+\frac{1}{y}}$
$x\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=3-e$

subject: Mathematics
solution:

1-x2y2dx=ydx+xdy

⇒dx=ydx+xdy1-xy2

⇒dx=dyx1-xy2

⇒dx=12dxy1-xy+dxy1+xy

Now integrating both side we get,

⇒2x+c=ln1+xy1-xy

⇒xy+1xy-1=ece2x

Now given, y1=2 so putting the value in above equation we get,

3=ece2

⇒ec=3e2

Hence, the equation becomes xy+1xy-1=3e2x-2

Now finding y2 so putting x=2 in above equation we get, 

2y+12y-1=3e2

⇒2y+1=2·3e2y-3e2

⇒1+3e2=2y3e2-1

⇒y2=1+3e223e2-1

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given: (2x+3y-2)dx+(4x+6y-7)dy=0

⇒dydx=-2x+3y-24x+6y-7   ...i

Let, 2x+3y-2=t

⇒2+3dydx=dtdx   ...ii

Also, 4x+6y-4=2t

⇒4x+6y-7=2t-3   ...iii

Using i, ii and iii,

⇒13dtdx-2=-t2t-3

⇒dtdx-2=-3t2t-3

⇒dtdx=-3t2t-3+2

⇒dtdx=-3t+4t-62t-3

⇒dtdx=t-62t-3

⇒∫2t-3t-6dt=∫dx

⇒∫2t-12t-6+9t-6·dt=x+c

⇒2t+9log(t-6)=x+c

⇒2(2x+3y-2)+9log(2x+3y-8)=x+c   ...iv

It is given that y(0)=3.

⇒2(0+9-2)+9log(0+9-8)=0+c

⇒14+9log(1)=c

⇒c=14

Using iv,

⇒4x+6y-4+9log(2x+3y-8)=x+14

⇒3x+6y+9log(2x+3y-8)=18

⇒x+2y+3log(2x+3y-8)=6

Comparing with αx+βy+3loge|2x+3y-γ|=6.

⇒α=1, β=2, γ=8

⇒α+2β+3γ=1+4+24

⇒α+2β+3γ=29

So, lny=-Klnx+ln2

Now it also passes through 8,1

⇒ln1=-Kln8+ln2⇒K=13

So, equation becomes lny=-13lnx+ln2

So, at x=18

⇒lny=-13ln18+ln2=2ln2

⇒y=4

At x=3, then

1-y32=2e3

Now an equation i becomes, 

lnB2B1=12ln65t2-t1   ...ii

Given that, at t2=kln2, B2=2000 & t=0, B1=1000

From ii, ln20001000=12ln65kln65-0=12k

⇒k=2ln2

∴ kln22=2ln2ln22=4

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The given differential equation is:

$(x^2 - x\sqrt{x^2 - 1})dy + (y(x - \sqrt{x^2 - 1}) - x)dx = 0$

Dividing the entire equation by $dx$ and rearranging, we get:

$x(x - \sqrt{x^2 - 1}) \dfrac{dy}{dx} + y(x - \sqrt{x^2 - 1}) = x$

Dividing by $x(x - \sqrt{x^2 - 1})$, we obtain a linear differential equation:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx} + \dfrac{1}{x}y = \dfrac{1}{x - \sqrt{x^2 - 1}}$

The integrating factor (I.F.) is:

$\text{I.F.} = e^{\int \dfrac{1}{x} dx} = e^{\ln x} = x$

Multiplying the differential equation by the integrating factor $x$, we get:

$x \dfrac{dy}{dx} + y = \dfrac{x}{x - \sqrt{x^2 - 1}}$

$\dfrac{d}{dx}(xy) = \dfrac{x(x + \sqrt{x^2 - 1})}{(x - \sqrt{x^2 - 1})(x + \sqrt{x^2 - 1})}$

$\dfrac{d}{dx}(xy) = \dfrac{x^2 + x\sqrt{x^2 - 1}}{x^2 - (x^2 - 1)}$

$\dfrac{d}{dx}(xy) = x^2 + x\sqrt{x^2 - 1}$

Integrating both sides with respect to $x$:

$\int d(xy) = \int (x^2 + x\sqrt{x^2 - 1}) dx$

$xy = \dfrac{x^3}{3} + \dfrac{(x^2 - 1)^{3/2}}{3} + C$

Given $y(1) = 1$, we substitute $x = 1$ and $y = 1$:

$1(1) = \dfrac{1^3}{3} + 0 + C \Rightarrow C = 1 - \dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{2}{3}$

So, the particular solution is:

$xy = \dfrac{x^3}{3} + \dfrac{(x^2 - 1)^{3/2}}{3} + \dfrac{2}{3}$

To find $y(\sqrt{5})$, we substitute $x = \sqrt{5}$:

$\sqrt{5} y(\sqrt{5}) = \dfrac{(\sqrt{5})^3}{3} + \dfrac{(5 - 1)^{3/2}}{3} + \dfrac{2}{3}$

$\sqrt{5} y(\sqrt{5}) = \dfrac{5\sqrt{5}}{3} + \dfrac{4^{3/2}}{3} + \dfrac{2}{3}$

$\sqrt{5} y(\sqrt{5}) = \dfrac{5\sqrt{5}}{3} + \dfrac{8}{3} + \dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{5\sqrt{5} + 10}{3}$

$y(\sqrt{5}) = \dfrac{5}{3} + \dfrac{10}{3\sqrt{5}} = \dfrac{5 + 2\sqrt{5}}{3}$

Since $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$, we have $2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.472$.

$y(\sqrt{5}) \approx \dfrac{5 + 4.472}{3} = \dfrac{9.472}{3} \approx 3.157$

The greatest integer less than $y(\sqrt{5})$ is $3$.

Answer: $3$

$\begin{aligned} & \left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}+1\right) \cos \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}} \sin \mathrm{x} d \mathrm{y}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{d}\left(\left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}+1\right) \sin \mathrm{x}\right)=0 \\ & \left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}+1\right) \sin \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$
It passes through $\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, 0\right)$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=2$ Now, $x=\frac{\pi}{6}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=3$

subject: Mathematics
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{3 \sin x}{\cos x \ln \cos x} y=-\frac{\sin x}{\cos x(\ln \cos x)^2} \\ & \text { I.F. }=e^{-\int \frac{3 \tan x}{\ln \cos x} d x} \\ & \text { Let } \ln \cos x=t \\ & -\tan x d x=d t \\ & e^{3 \int^{\frac{d t}{t}}}=e^{3 \ln t}=t^3=(\ln \cos x)^3 \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Solution will be } \\ & \quad y(\ln \cos x)^3=-\int(\tan x)(\ln \cos x) d x \\ & y(\ln \cos x)^3=\frac{(\ln \cos x)^2}{2}+c \\ & \because \quad y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=-\frac{1}{\ln 2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad c=0 \\ & \therefore \quad y=\frac{1}{2(\ln \cos x)} \\ & y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{\ln \left(\cos \frac{\pi}{6}\right)} \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{\ln \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{\ln \sqrt{3}-\ln 2} \\ & =\frac{1}{\ln 3-\ln 4}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Rewriting: $\frac{dy}{dx} - \frac{2x}{x^2-4}y = -2x(x^2-4)$.
IF $= e^{-\int \frac{2x}{x^2-4}dx} = \frac{1}{x^2-4}$.
$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{y}{x^2-4}\right) = -2x \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x^2-4} = -x^2 + C$.
$y = (x^2-4)(C-x^2)$. At $(3,15)$: $15 = 5(C-9) \Rightarrow C = 12$.
$y = (x^2-4)(12-x^2)$.
$y' = 4x(8-x^2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2\sqrt{2}$ (for $x > 2$).
$y'' = 32 - 12x^2 = -64 < 0$ at $x = 2\sqrt{2}$, confirming local maximum.
$f(2\sqrt{2}) = (8-4)(12-8) = 16$.

Put $x=\pi, \tan ^{-1} e+\tan ^{-1} y=\frac{\pi}{2}$ $\tan ^{-1} y=\cot ^{-1} e$ $y=\frac{1}{e}$

⇒loge+(loge)22+tan-1y=π4

⇒y=1-tan321+tan32

Hence, on comparing we get,

⇒α=β=1

⇒α+2β=3

solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& 10 \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \int_1^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{t}) \mathrm{dt}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(5 \mathrm{xf}(\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{x}^5-9\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 10 f(x)=5 f(x)+5 \mathrm{xf}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})-5 \mathrm{x}^4 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x}^4=\mathrm{xf}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{x}^4=\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+y\left(-\frac{1}{x}\right)=x^3 \\ & \Rightarrow y e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}=\int x^3 e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}+c \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{ye}^{-\operatorname{tn}|x|}=\int \mathrm{x}^3 \mathrm{e}^{-\operatorname{tr}|x|}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{y}}{|\mathrm{x}|}=\int \frac{\mathrm{x}^3}{|\mathrm{x}|}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}=\int \mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{\mathrm{x}^3}{3}+\mathrm{c}
\end{aligned}$
Put $x=1$ in given equation
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 0=5 \mathrm{f}(1)-1-9 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(1)=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2}{1}=\frac{1}{3}+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\frac{5}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{f}(3)}{3}=\frac{27}{3}+\frac{5}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(3)=32
\end{aligned}$

-1y2dydx+1xy=32x2

∵   ye=e3⇒c=32    ⇒xy=32lnx+32 is the particular solution.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
I.F. $\mathrm{e}^{-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{1-\mathrm{x}^2} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(1-\mathrm{x}^2\right)}=\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^2}$

$y \times \sqrt{1-x^2}=\int\left(x^6+4 x\right) d x=\frac{x^7}{7}+2 x^2+c$

Given $y(0)=0 \Rightarrow c=0$
$y=\frac{\frac{x^7}{7}+2 x^2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}$

Now, $6 \int_{-\frac{1}{2}}^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{\frac{x^7}{7}+2 \mathrm{x}^2}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^2}} \mathrm{dx}=6 \int_{-\frac{1}{2}}^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{2 \mathrm{x}^2}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^2}} \mathrm{dx}$
$\begin{aligned} & =24 \int_0^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{x^2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x \\ & \text { Put } x=\sin \theta \\ & d x=\cos \theta d \theta \\ & =24 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \frac{\sin ^2 \theta}{\cos \theta} \cos \theta d \theta \\ & =24 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{6}}\left(\frac{1-\cos 2 \theta}{2}\right) d \theta=12\left[\theta-\frac{\sin 2 \theta}{2}\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \\ & =12\left(\frac{\pi}{6}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) \\ & =2 \pi-3 \sqrt{3} \\ & \alpha^2=(3 \sqrt{3})^2=27\end{aligned}$

Integrating factor I.F=e∫Pdx=e∫1xlogxdx=e∫1/xlogxdx=eloglogx=logx

The solution of the equation becomes 

y·e∫Pdx=∫Q·e∫Pdxdx+c, where, c is the constant of integration.

⇒ylog x=∫2log xdx+c

⇒ylog x=2x log x-x+c

Let, P1,y1 be any point on the curve.

⇒y1·0=20-1+c⇒c=2

⇒ylogx=2xlogx-x+2

Now, putting x=e, we get, y=2.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given dydx+3sec2xy=sec2x

This is linear differential equation, of the type dydx+Py=Q, where P & Q are functions of x or constants.

Here, P=3sec2x & Q=sec2x

Now, integrating factor I.F.=e∫Pdx

=e∫3sec2x dx=e3tanx

Hence, the solution of the differential equation is yI.F.=∫QI.F.dx+c

⇒y·e3tanx=∫e3tanx·sec2xdx

Let, tanx=t,  ⇒sec2xdx=dt

⇒y·e3tanx=∫e3tdt+c

⇒y·e3tanx=e3t3+c

⇒y·e3tanx=e3tanx3+c

⇒y=ce-3tanx+13

Given, yπ4=43⇒

43=ce-3+13

⇒c=e3

Thus, y=e3·e-3tanx+13

Hence, y-π4=e3·e-3tan-π4+13

⇒y-π4=e3·e-3-1+13

=e3·e3+13=e6+13.

2x+2ydydx-2kdydx=0

From equation 1,

⇒x+y-x2+y22ydydx=0

⇒x2-y2dydx=2xy=gxy

⇒gx=2x

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Differentiating $6\int_1^x f(t)dt = 3xf(x) + x^3 - 4$:
$6f(x) = 3f(x) + 3xf'(x) + 3x^2 \Rightarrow f(x) - xf'(x) = x^2$.
$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{f(x)}{x}\right) = \frac{xf'(x)-f(x)}{x^2} = -1$.
Integrating: $\frac{f(x)}{x} = -x + C \Rightarrow f(x) = -x^2 + Cx$.
At $x = 1$: $0 = 3f(1) - 3 \Rightarrow f(1) = 1$. So $-1 + C = 1 \Rightarrow C = 2$.
$f(x) = -x^2 + 2x$. $f(2) = 0$, $f(3) = -3$.
$f(2) - f(3) = 3$.

solution:
$\frac{d(p(t))}{d t}=\frac{1}{2} p(t)-450$
$\frac{d(p(t))}{d t}=\frac{p(t)-900}{2}$
$2 \int \frac{d(p(t))}{p(t)-900}=\int d t$
$2 \ln |p(t)-900|=t+c$ 
$t=0 \quad \Rightarrow 2 \ln 50=0+c \quad \Rightarrow c=2 \ln 50$
$\therefore 2 \ln |\mathrm{p}(\mathrm{t})-900|=\mathrm{t}+2 \ln 50$
$P(\mathrm{t})=0 \quad \Rightarrow 2 \ln 900=\mathrm{t}+2 \ln 50$
$t=2(\ln 900-\ln 50)=2 \ln \left(\frac{900}{50}\right)=2 \ln 18$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The given differential equation can be rewritten as:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx} + (\tan x)y = \dfrac{\sec^3 x}{(\tan x)^{1/2}}$

This is a linear differential equation of the form $\dfrac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x)$.

Integrating Factor (IF) = $e^{\int \tan x \, dx} = e^{\ln(\sec x)} = \sec x$

Multiplying both sides by the IF and integrating, we get:

$y \sec x = \int \dfrac{\sec^4 x}{(\tan x)^{1/2}} \, dx + C$

$y \sec x = \int \dfrac{(1 + \tan^2 x)\sec^2 x}{(\tan x)^{1/2}} \, dx + C$

Let $\tan x = t$, then $\sec^2 x \, dx = dt$.

$y \sec x = \int \dfrac{1 + t^2}{t^{1/2}} \, dt + C$

$y \sec x = \int (t^{-1/2} + t^{3/2}) \, dt + C$

$y \sec x = 2t^{1/2} + \dfrac{2}{5}t^{5/2} + C$

$y \sec x = 2(\tan x)^{1/2} + \dfrac{2}{5}(\tan x)^{5/2} + C$

Given $y\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = \dfrac{6\sqrt{2}}{5}$, substituting $x = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$:

$\dfrac{6\sqrt{2}}{5} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 2(1)^{1/2} + \dfrac{2}{5}(1)^{5/2} + C$

$\dfrac{12}{5} = 2 + \dfrac{2}{5} + C \Rightarrow C = 0$

The solution is $y \sec x = 2(\tan x)^{1/2} + \dfrac{2}{5}(\tan x)^{5/2}$.

Substituting $x = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$:

$y\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot 2 = 2(\sqrt{3})^{1/2} + \dfrac{2}{5}(\sqrt{3})^{5/2}$

$y\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = 3^{1/4} + \dfrac{1}{5} \cdot 3^{5/4} = 3^{1/4}\left(1 + \dfrac{3}{5}\right) = \dfrac{8}{5} \cdot 3^{1/4}$

Given $y\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = \dfrac{4}{5}\alpha$, we have:

$\dfrac{4}{5}\alpha = \dfrac{8}{5} \cdot 3^{1/4} \Rightarrow \alpha = 2 \cdot 3^{1/4}$

Therefore, $\alpha^4 = (2 \cdot 3^{1/4})^4 = 16 \cdot 3 = 48$

Answer: $48$

⇒∫3v2+1dvv3+v=∫dxx

⇒lnv3+v=lnx+c

⇒lnyx3+yx=lnx+c

Given, y1=1

⇒c=ln2

So the equation becomes lnyx3+yx=lnx+ln2

Now for y2

So putting x=2 in lnyx3+yx=lnx+ln2

 We get, lny38+y2=2ln2⇒y38+y2=4

⇒y2=2

So, y2∈[2,3)

So on comparing with y2∈[n-1,n)

⇒n=3
